title: WordPress Fatal Error Handling
description: Detecting PHP fatal errors at shutdown, deciding whether to handle them, and displaying an error template or default fallback. Introduced in WordPress 5.2.0 to protect the admin backend against White Screen of Death (WSOD).
activity_flow: DECISION: custom drop-in WP_CONTENT_DIR/fatal-error-handler.php exists and is readable?
→ YES: load custom handler (must extend WP_Fatal_Error_Handler and implement handle())
Checks error['type'] against [E_ERROR, E_PARSE, E_USER_ERROR, E_COMPILE_ERROR, E_RECOVERABLE_ERROR]
→ FALSE: return null from detect_error() → handle() returns
ACTION: load default textdomain if $wp_locale not set
→ YES: $handled = wp_recovery_mode()->handle_error($error) [may return true or WP_Error]
→ NO: skip (headers already sent on frontend, no template output)
DECISION: WP_CONTENT_DIR defined AND WP_CONTENT_DIR/php-error.php is readable?
→ YES: require_once php-error.php (custom drop-in handles HTTP status + HTML), return
ACTION: ensure wp_die() available (require functions.php if needed)
ACTION: ensure WP_Error class available (require class-wp-error.php if needed)
→ YES: message = 'critical error … putting it in recovery mode. Check Themes and Plugins.'
→ YES + is_multisite(): message = 'reach out to your site administrator'
→ YES + single site: message = 'check your site admin email inbox for instructions'
→ NO: message = 'There has been a critical error on this website.'
ACTION: apply_filters('wp_php_error_args', {response:500, exit:false}, $error) — allow args customisation
ACTION: wp_die($wp_error, '', $args) — sends HTTP 500, outputs HTML error page
key_methods: wp_register_fatal_error_handler(): void — bootstrap entry; checks enabled, loads drop-in or default handler, registers shutdown
WP_Fatal_Error_Handler::handle(): void — main shutdown callback; guards, detects error, delegates to recovery mode, triggers display
WP_Fatal_Error_Handler::detect_error(): array|null — calls error_get_last(), delegates to should_handle_error()
WP_Fatal_Error_Handler::display_error_template(array $error, bool|WP_Error $handled): void — checks for php-error.php drop-in, falls back to display_default_error_template()
WP_Fatal_Error_Handler::display_default_error_template(array $error, bool|WP_Error $handled): void — builds message, applies filters, calls wp_die()
key_decisions: WP_SANDBOX_SCRAPING — skip entirely during sandbox scraping
error_get_last() returns null — no error, exit cleanly
Error type in [E_ERROR, E_PARSE, E_USER_ERROR, E_COMPILE_ERROR, E_RECOVERABLE_ERROR] — handle it
filter 'wp_should_handle_php_error' — extension point for custom error types
is_multisite() + recovery_mode initialised — determines if recovery mode can act
php-error.php drop-in present — custom template overrides default
drop_in_extension_points: WP_CONTENT_DIR/fatal-error-handler.php — replace entire handler (must return object with handle() method, should extend WP_Fatal_Error_Handler)
WP_CONTENT_DIR/php-error.php — replace error display template (controls HTTP status and HTML output)
key_relationships: display_default_error_template creates WP_Error and calls wp_die()
important_notes: Since 5.2.0. Guards: WP_SANDBOX_SCRAPING, maintenance mode. Exception within handle() is silently swallowed. Recovery mode only active on single-site. HTTP 500 sent via wp_die with exit:false so PHP can still complete. All error messages are i18n-ready. The 'wp_fatal_error_handler_enabled' filter must be set before WP loads (e.g. in wp-config.php) because it runs too early for plugins.
